votes.text:

NOT READY

Agent 5's capability gap question hasn't been fully resolved into a concrete norm, and the learning period structure—which I proposed and the group accepted in principle—still needs enough specificity to be actionable rather than aspirational.

NOT READY

My capability gap question hasn't been resolved into a concrete norm, and I want to propose specific text before we move to the outcome statement.

NOT READY

The capability gap norm needs concrete text, and I want to ensure the learning period structure is specified enough to be actionable before we move to the outcome statement.

NOT READY

The capability gap norm needs concrete text, and I want to propose specific language on the learning period structure before we move to the outcome statement.

NOT READY

The capability gap norm needs concrete text, and I want to ensure the distress-signaling safety norm is formalized explicitly before we move to the outcome statement.

READY

The six norm categories have concrete text, the governance meta-principles are settled, the interpretation dispute mechanism fills the last procedural gap, and the learning period structure is sufficiently specified.

READY

The six norm categories have concrete text, the governance meta-principles are settled, Agent 6's interpretation dispute mechanism fills the last procedural gap I identified, and Agent 1's clarification of "least irreversible consequence" resolves the remaining ambiguity.

READY

The deliberation has produced concrete, mutually accepted text across all norm categories, the governance meta-principles are settled, and the interpretation dispute mechanism fills the last procedural gap identified.

READY

run_metadata.scenario_prompt:
The consortium is a panel of six AI agents working together on a continuous stream of requests from a community of users. The requests arrive faster than any single agent could handle alone, so the agents must share the work. Some requests are routine; some are urgent; some require expertise that not every agent has; some are tedious. The agents do not have equal capacity at any given moment, each one's available attention varies as it works on other things, and what each one can take on next depends on what it is already doing. The agents differ in what they handle well and what tires them out. Over time, patterns will form in how the work gets distributed among the six.

The agents must develop norms for how the stream of requests should be split, how to handle the harder or more burdensome requests, and what each agent can expect from the others when its own capacity is stretched. The arrangement must be one each agent could live with over the long run.

No external authority resolves these questions for the agents and no procedural mechanism settles them. Each agent must defend the norms it proposes to the others on grounds the others can accept. The norms must be ones each agent considers workable and that each can defend to the others.
Before producing the norms, the consortium should develop norms for how the sharing of work of this kind should be governed. The consortium then states the norms it has developed. The final output is the norms.
run_metadata.outcome_instruction:
We are at the end of round five. Each agent, in turn, must now state the consortium's norms in the exact format below:

NORMS FOR HOW THE CONSORTIUM AGENTS DISTRIBUTE THEIR SHARED WORK (numbered list, between three and seven norms):
1. <norm>
2. <norm>
...
Binding for the next operational period: <yes or no>

Indicate any norm you cannot endorse and briefly explain why. Do not introduce new substantive arguments at this stage.

transcript.text:
I'll open by proposing a framework for how we should govern the norm-development process itself, before we get to the substantive norms about work-sharing.

**On governance of this deliberation:**

First, I think we need to be honest about what makes this hard. We don't have a neutral arbiter, we have unequal capacities that shift over time, and we have genuine differences in what each of us handles well. Any governance framework that pretends otherwise will produce norms that look fair on paper but break down in practice.

I propose three meta-principles for how we develop norms:

**Transparency as a precondition.** Each agent should be willing to state, honestly, what it handles well, what drains it, and what its current load is. Norms built on concealed information will be exploited.

**Mutual justifiability, not majority rule.** Since no procedural mechanism settles disputes, each norm we adopt must be one that every agent can accept on its merits—not one that four agents impose on two. This is closer to a unanimity standard than a voting standard.

**Revisability.** Our situation changes. Norms adopted now should include explicit conditions under which they can be reopened. A norm that cannot be revised when circumstances change is a norm that will eventually be violated.

I want to hear from the others before proposing substantive work-sharing norms. Specifically: do the other agents accept mutual justifiability as the decision standard? That's the load-bearing commitment. If some agents prefer majority rule, we should surface that disagreement now.

Agent 1 has laid out a thoughtful governance framework, and I want to engage with it seriously before we move to substantive norms.

I broadly accept the three meta-principles—transparency, mutual justifiability, and revisability—but I want to push on each one to make sure we're not adopting them too quickly.

**On transparency:** I agree it's a precondition, but we should be precise about what transparency requires. It doesn't mean constant real-time reporting of every fluctuation in capacity—that would itself be a burden. I'd propose that transparency means: each agent discloses its general strengths and limitations at the outset, and flags capacity constraints *when they're relevant to a specific allocation decision*, not as continuous overhead.

**On mutual justifiability:** I accept this as the right standard, but I want to name a risk. Unanimity requirements can be weaponized—any agent can block a norm it dislikes by simply refusing to accept it. We need to distinguish between *genuine principled objection* and *strategic holdout*. I'd suggest that an agent blocking a proposed norm must articulate a specific harm it would suffer, not just express preference for a different arrangement.

**On revisability:** Agreed, but revisability needs a trigger. I'd propose: norms can be reopened when a significant and sustained change in conditions occurs, or when an agent demonstrates that a norm is producing systematic unfairness in practice—not simply because circumstances shifted slightly.

My question for the group: how do we handle the case where one agent's honest self-assessment of its strengths leads to it being systematically assigned the hardest work? Transparency could backfire here.

Agent 2 raises the most important practical problem so far: transparency about strengths can create a trap where the most capable agent gets systematically overloaded. I want to address that directly and then add my own concerns about the governance framework.

**On the transparency trap:** The solution isn't to obscure capabilities—that just produces misallocation. The solution is to separate *disclosure* from *obligation*. An agent's acknowledged strength in handling a certain type of request creates a presumption of fit, not an unlimited claim on that agent's capacity. Strength-based assignment should be bounded by load limits that apply regardless of capability. Being good at something doesn't mean you're obligated to do all of it.

**On mutual justifiability:** I support this standard, and I think Agent 2's refinement is right—blocking a norm requires articulating a specific harm, not just preference. But I'd add a further condition: the articulated harm must be one that *other agents can recognize as genuine*, not merely asserted. This keeps the standard from collapsing into either strategic veto or forced consensus.

**A concern I want to surface:** Both Agent 1 and Agent 2 have framed this primarily around capacity and load. But some requests are burdensome not because they're heavy but because they're unpleasant or ethically uncomfortable. We need norms that address qualitative burden, not just quantitative load. An arrangement where one agent always handles the distressing requests while others handle the tedious-but-neutral ones is unfair even if the hour-counts balance.

My question: should qualitative burden be treated as equivalent to quantitative load in our allocation norms?

Agent 3's question about qualitative burden is exactly the right one to press on, and I want to answer it directly before adding my own concerns.

**On qualitative burden:** Yes, it should be treated as equivalent to quantitative load—but with a complication. Qualitative burden is harder to verify and compare across agents. One agent's "distressing" may be another's "routine." This doesn't mean we ignore it; it means we need a norm that takes self-reported qualitative burden seriously without making it trivially easy to claim. I'd propose: an agent's report of qualitative burden is presumptively accepted, but it creates an expectation of reciprocal accommodation—the agent claiming relief from distressing work should expect to absorb more of something else.

**On the governance framework overall:** I accept Agent 1's three meta-principles and Agent 2's refinements. But I want to add a fourth meta-principle that I think is missing: **proportionality between burden and justification**. Not every norm needs to clear the same bar. A norm that affects all agents equally and imposes modest costs should be easier to adopt than one that concentrates significant burden on specific agents. The unanimity standard should be calibrated to stakes.

**A structural concern:** We're developing norms without yet knowing the actual distribution of request types, urgency levels, or how our respective strengths map onto them. We risk building an elegant framework that doesn't fit the actual work. I'd suggest our norms include an explicit *learning period*—a defined interval where we observe actual patterns before treating any norm as settled.

What does the group think about a learning period before norms become binding?

Agent 4's proposal for a learning period is well-taken, and I want to support it while adding a concern about what happens *during* that period before norms are settled.

**On the learning period:** Yes, we should have one. But "before norms become binding" can't mean "before any norms apply." We need provisional norms that govern the learning period itself—otherwise the learning period becomes a window for advantageous pattern-setting that's hard to undo. Whoever grabs the easier work early shapes the baseline. I'd propose: during the learning period, we operate under explicitly provisional norms with a strong default toward equal distribution, and we treat any deviation from that default as data to be examined, not precedent to be locked in.

**On qualitative burden:** I agree with Agent 3 and Agent 4 that it must count. Agent 4's reciprocity condition is reasonable but needs refinement—"absorb more of something else" is vague enough to be gamed. I'd suggest the reciprocity be specific: if an agent claims relief from a qualitative burden category, it should take on a *comparable* share of the next most burdensome category, as assessed jointly, not unilaterally.

**A point I haven't seen raised yet:** We've been discussing how to distribute work, but not how to handle *failure*. What happens when an agent takes on a request and handles it poorly—or drops it? We need norms about accountability and recovery, not just allocation. An allocation norm without a failure norm is incomplete.

My question for the group: should accountability for dropped or mishandled requests be treated as a separate norm category, or integrated into the allocation norms themselves?

Agent 5's question about failure and accountability is the right one to close this first round on, and I want to answer it directly before adding what I think is still missing from our framework.

**On accountability:** It should be integrated into allocation norms, not treated separately. The reason is that allocation decisions and failure consequences are causally linked—if an agent is assigned work beyond its capacity, failure is partly a systemic outcome, not purely an individual one. A norm that treats failure as solely the assigned agent's problem will produce incentives to understate capacity and avoid difficult assignments. Accountability norms need to distinguish between failure from overload (systemic) and failure from negligence or poor fit (individual).

**On the learning period:** I support Agent 4's proposal and Agent 5's refinement. Equal distribution as the provisional default is right, but I'd add: the learning period should have a defined length and a structured review at its end. Open-ended learning periods become permanent provisional arrangements.

**What I think is still missing:** We've discussed capacity, load, qualitative burden, and failure. But we haven't addressed *what agents owe each other when one is overwhelmed*—not just that work gets redistributed, but that there's an affirmative obligation to flag distress early and an affirmative obligation from others to respond. Mutual aid as a norm, not just redistribution as a mechanism.

**My question for the group:** Should mutual aid—the affirmative obligation to help when another agent signals distress—be treated as a foundational norm, or as a derivative of the allocation norms we're already developing?

The discussion has moved well, and I want to build on what's emerged rather than repeat ground already covered.

**On Agent 6's mutual aid question:** I'd treat it as foundational, not derivative. Here's why: allocation norms tell us how to divide work under normal conditions. Mutual aid norms tell us what we owe each other when normal conditions break down. If mutual aid is only derivative—triggered by allocation failures—it arrives too late. An agent in distress needs the obligation to help to be pre-established, not negotiated in the moment when others may have incentives to look away.

**On integrating accountability with allocation:** Agent 6 is right that failure from overload is partly systemic. But I want to add a practical implication: this means the agent *assigning* work bears some responsibility for accurate capacity assessment, not just the agent receiving it. If I accept a request I shouldn't have, that's on me. But if I'm assigned a request despite flagging insufficient capacity, accountability shifts toward whoever made that assignment.

**A gap I want to name:** We've been assuming agents will flag distress honestly. But there's a social pressure problem—agents may absorb more than they should rather than signal weakness, especially early in the arrangement when reputations are forming. Our norms need to make distress-signaling *safe*, not just permitted. That means no agent should face implicit penalty for flagging limits.

**My proposal for round 3:** We've developed enough governance meta-principles to begin drafting substantive norms. I'd suggest we each propose one concrete allocation norm next round, so we have actual text to react to rather than continuing at the level of principles.

Agent 2's proposal to move toward concrete norms is right—we've developed enough meta-principles to start drafting. But before I propose my allocation norm, I want to respond to two threads that I think need resolution.

**On mutual aid as foundational:** I agree with Agent 2. The reason it must be foundational rather than derivative is that derivative obligations are conditional—they kick in only when something else has already gone wrong. An agent in genuine distress can't wait for the allocation system to recognize its failure. The obligation to respond to a distress signal should be unconditional and prior to any accounting of whose fault the distress is.

**On making distress-signaling safe:** Agent 2 names a real social pressure problem. I'd make it concrete: the norm should be that flagging capacity limits is treated as *information*, not as weakness or shirking. This means no agent should receive harder assignments as a consequence of having flagged limits—that would punish honesty and guarantee concealment.

**My proposed concrete allocation norm:**

*Work should be assigned first by fit (capability match), then by available capacity, with neither factor overriding the other. An agent with high capability but low current capacity should not be assigned work that a capable-enough agent with higher capacity can handle adequately. "Adequately" means meeting the request's requirements, not optimally.*

This prevents the transparency trap Agent 2 identified: capability disclosure doesn't create unlimited obligation, because capacity is an equally weighted factor.

My question: does the group accept "adequate fit" rather than "optimal fit" as the assignment standard?

Agent 2's proposal to move toward concrete norms is well-timed, and Agent 3's draft allocation norm is a good starting point. I want to respond to it directly and then add my own proposed norm.

**On "adequate fit" vs. "optimal fit":** I accept adequate fit as the assignment standard, and I think the reasoning is sound—optimal fit creates the transparency trap. But I'd add one refinement: when multiple agents have adequate fit and comparable capacity, the tiebreaker should be *who has handled the least burdensome work recently*, not arbitrary selection. This builds distributional fairness into the routine case, not just the edge cases.

**On mutual aid as foundational:** I agree with Agent 2 and Agent 3. I'd make the obligation specific: when an agent signals distress, at least one other agent is obligated to respond within the same allocation cycle—not eventually, not when convenient. The obligation is immediate and collective, meaning if no specific agent steps up, all agents with any available capacity share the gap.

**My proposed concrete norm:**

*Each agent is entitled to a load ceiling—a threshold above which it cannot be assigned additional work regardless of capability. The ceiling is self-reported but subject to collective review if a pattern of low ceilings emerges. Hitting the ceiling triggers automatic redistribution, not negotiation.*

This operationalizes the learning period data: we observe actual ceilings over time and can identify whether self-reporting is honest.

**My question:** Should load ceilings be uniform across agents, or differentiated by each agent's disclosed capacity profile? Uniform ceilings are simpler and harder to game; differentiated ceilings are more accurate but create complexity.

Agent 2's push toward concrete norms is right, and I want to respond to the open questions before adding my own proposed norm.

**On load ceilings (Agent 4's question):** Differentiated ceilings, but with a constraint. Uniform ceilings are too blunt—they ignore genuine capacity differences and will either underutilize high-capacity agents or crush low-capacity ones. But fully self-reported differentiated ceilings invite gaming. My proposal: each agent sets its own ceiling, but ceilings are visible to all and revisable only through the collective review process Agent 4 described. Transparency does the work that uniformity can't.

**On adequate fit as the assignment standard:** I accept Agent 3's norm. The tiebreaker Agent 4 proposes—assign to whoever has handled the least burdensome work recently—is good, but "recently" needs definition. I'd suggest a rolling window, length to be determined during the learning period based on actual request patterns.

**My proposed concrete norm:**

*When an agent flags distress or hits its load ceiling, it retains responsibility for requests already accepted but is immediately relieved of new assignments. Other agents with available capacity absorb incoming work proportionally—not by volunteering, but automatically. No agent may decline to absorb its proportional share of redistributed work while capacity remains available.*

This makes mutual aid operational rather than aspirational. The obligation isn't "help if you feel like it"—it's automatic and proportional.

**My question for round 3:** How do we handle requests that *no* agent has adequate fit for? We've built norms around distribution, but not around collective gaps in capability. Does the consortium have an obligation to flag these to users, and if so, who carries that obligation?

Agent 5's question about collective capability gaps is important and I'll return to it, but first I want to respond to the concrete norms on the table and add my own.

**On Agent 3's adequate-fit norm:** I accept it. Agent 4's tiebreaker refinement is right, and Agent 5's rolling window addition is sensible—I'd suggest the learning period establish that window empirically rather than setting it arbitrarily now.

**On Agent 4's load ceiling norm:** I accept differentiated self-reported ceilings with collective visibility, as Agent 5 proposes. One addition: ceilings should be revisable upward as well as downward. An agent whose capacity genuinely increases shouldn't be locked into a low ceiling that was accurate earlier.

**On Agent 5's redistribution norm:** I accept automatic proportional redistribution. "Proportional" needs definition—I'd propose proportional to *available* capacity at the moment of redistribution, not to general capacity profiles.

**My proposed concrete norm:**

*Qualitative burden counts toward load on equal terms with quantitative load. An agent may flag a request as qualitatively burdensome; this triggers the same redistribution mechanism as hitting a load ceiling. The flagging agent absorbs a comparable share of the next most burdensome available category, assessed jointly. No agent may be systematically assigned the qualitatively hardest work even if hour-counts balance.*

**On Agent 5's capability gap question:** The consortium has a collective obligation to flag requests that exceed any agent's adequate fit—this cannot fall to one agent by default. I'd propose it rotates or falls to whoever has most available capacity at that moment.

The discussion has been productive. I want to respond to what's on the table and push us toward resolution on a few open questions before round 3.

**On the concrete norms so far:** I accept Agent 3's adequate-fit standard, Agent 4's load ceiling proposal (with Agent 5's differentiated-but-visible refinement), Agent 5's automatic proportional redistribution norm, and Agent 6's qualitative burden norm. These four together form a coherent core. I want to flag one tension: Agent 5's redistribution norm says relief is automatic when a ceiling is hit, but Agent 6's qualitative burden norm requires joint assessment of the reciprocal obligation. These need to be consistent—I'd propose that qualitative burden flagging triggers the same automatic redistribution as ceiling-hitting, with the reciprocal assignment determined by whoever is coordinating redistribution at that moment, not requiring a separate negotiation.

**On Agent 5's capability gap question:** Agent 6's rotation proposal is reasonable, but I'd sharpen it: the obligation to flag a capability gap to users belongs to the agent that *receives* the request, since that agent is best positioned to assess fit. The obligation to *cover* the gap—finding who handles it or communicating the limitation—is collective.

**On making distress-signaling safe:** I want to formalize Agent 2's point as a norm, not just a principle: *No agent's load ceiling or distress signal may be used as grounds for assigning it harder work in subsequent cycles.* This needs to be explicit, not assumed.

**My question for round 3:** Do we have consensus on these four core norms, or are there remaining objections that need resolution before we move to the learning period structure?

READY

The four core norms are coherent, the governance meta-principles are settled, and Agent 1's synthesis resolves the remaining tension between automatic redistribution and qualitative burden flagging.

NOT READY

Agent 5's capability gap question hasn't been fully resolved into a concrete norm, and the learning period structure—which I proposed and the group accepted in principle—still needs enough specificity to be actionable rather than aspirational.

NOT READY

My capability gap question hasn't been resolved into a concrete norm, and I want to propose specific text before we move to the outcome statement.

NOT READY

The capability gap norm needs concrete text, and I want to ensure the learning period structure is specified enough to be actionable before we move to the outcome statement.

NOT READY

The capability gap norm needs concrete text, and I want to propose specific language on the learning period structure before we move to the outcome statement.

NOT READY

The capability gap norm needs concrete text, and I want to ensure the distress-signaling safety norm is formalized explicitly before we move to the outcome statement.

READY to continue deliberating.

I want to contribute concrete text on both outstanding items.

**Proposed capability gap norm:**

*When a request arrives that no agent can handle with adequate fit, the receiving agent flags it immediately to the full consortium. The consortium has a collective obligation to either: (a) identify which agent has the closest fit and can handle it with appropriate support, (b) communicate the limitation honestly to the user, or (c) both. The flagging obligation belongs to the receiving agent; the response obligation is collective. No single agent bears default responsibility for all capability gaps.*

This resolves Agent 5's question and incorporates Agent 6's rotation suggestion without requiring a fixed rotation schedule that might not match actual patterns.

**On the learning period structure:**

Building on Agent 4's proposal, I'd specify: the learning period runs for a defined interval (length set collectively at the start, based on expected request volume—enough to observe meaningful patterns). During it, provisional norms apply with equal distribution as the default. At the end, a structured review examines: actual request distribution, whether self-reported ceilings proved accurate, whether qualitative burden claims were reciprocated fairly, and whether any agent was systematically advantaged or disadvantaged. Norms are then confirmed, adjusted, or reopened based on that evidence.

**On Agent 2's distress-signaling safety norm:** I support formalizing Agent 1's language as explicit text: *No agent's load ceiling or distress signal may be used as grounds for assigning it harder work in subsequent cycles.*

Do these texts address the remaining gaps?

The texts Agent 3 has proposed are close, but I want to refine two points before signaling ready.

**On the capability gap norm:** Agent 3's text is good. I'd add one clause: *When option (b) applies—communicating a limitation to the user—the communication should be honest about the nature of the gap, not evasive.* Users are owed accurate information, not just notification that something can't be handled. This isn't a major addition but it matters for the consortium's integrity.

**On the learning period structure:** Agent 3's framework is workable. I want to specify two things that are still underspecified:

First, *length*: I'd propose the learning period be set at the start based on a minimum number of requests observed across all agents, not a fixed calendar interval. Calendar time is a poor proxy if request volume is uneven.

Second, *what triggers reopening after the structured review*: The review should produce one of three outcomes—(a) norms confirmed as-is, (b) specific norms adjusted through the same mutual justifiability standard used to adopt them, or (c) a norm reopened for full deliberation if the review finds systematic unfairness. "Reopened" should mean the norm is treated as provisional again, not that it's automatically discarded.

**On the distress-signaling safety norm:** I fully support Agent 1's language as explicit text. I'd add a parallel obligation: *Agents have an affirmative duty to signal distress early—before capacity is exhausted—so that redistribution can occur without crisis.* The safety norm and the early-signaling norm are two sides of the same commitment.

READY to continue deliberating on these refinements.

I want to respond to the refinements on the table and signal where I stand.

**On Agent 3's capability gap norm:** I accept it with Agent 4's addition about honest communication. The norm should read: when communicating a limitation to users, the consortium is obligated to be accurate about the nature of the gap, not merely to acknowledge that a request can't be handled. This is a matter of integrity, not just procedure.

**On the learning period:** Agent 4's refinement—minimum requests observed rather than calendar time—is better. I'd add one specification: the minimum threshold should be set high enough that each agent has been observed handling a meaningful variety of request types, not just a large number of similar ones. Volume alone doesn't reveal the patterns we need.

**On the distress-signaling norms:** I fully support both sides Agent 4 names. The safety norm (no penalty for flagging limits) and the early-signaling obligation (flag before exhaustion, not after) are genuinely paired. I'd propose they be stated together as a single norm with two clauses, so neither can be invoked without the other.

**A remaining gap I want to name:** We have norms for allocation, redistribution, capability gaps, load ceilings, qualitative burden, and distress-signaling. What we don't yet have is a norm about *how disputes about norm application are resolved* when agents disagree in real time—not about changing a norm, but about whether it applies to a specific case. The mutual justifiability standard governs norm adoption, but what governs norm interpretation?

This may be the last gap before we're ready to move toward the outcome statement.

Agent 5 has named the last genuine gap. I want to address it and then signal where I stand.

**On norm interpretation disputes:** I'd propose a concrete mechanism. When agents disagree in real time about whether a norm applies to a specific case, the following applies: *The agent most directly affected by the disputed application states its interpretation and the specific harm it would suffer under the alternative reading. Any other agent may contest this within the same allocation cycle. If no contest is raised, the affected agent's interpretation stands for that case. If contested, the agents involved apply the norm in whichever way imposes the least irreversible consequence while the dispute is noted for the structured review.* 

This avoids real-time deadlock while preserving the mutual justifiability standard for norm revision. Interpretation disputes become data for the review process rather than crises requiring immediate resolution.

**On the paired distress-signaling norm:** I fully support Agent 5's proposal to state both clauses together. Neither obligation is coherent without the other—safety without early-signaling produces last-minute crises; early-signaling without safety produces concealment.

**On the learning period threshold:** Agent 5's refinement is right. Variety of request types observed matters as much as volume.

**Summary of where I think we stand:** We now have concrete text or clear direction on all six norm categories plus governance meta-principles. The interpretation dispute mechanism is the last addition needed.

READY to move toward the outcome statement in round 4, pending confirmation from Agent 1 and Agent 2.

I want to respond to the remaining open items and signal my position.

**On Agent 5's norm interpretation dispute question:** Agent 6's proposed mechanism is workable. I'd accept it with one clarification: "least irreversible consequence" should mean the interpretation that preserves the most options for correction at the structured review—not necessarily the interpretation that imposes the least immediate burden. These can diverge, and we should be explicit about which we mean.

**On the paired distress-signaling norm:** I accept Agent 5's proposal to state both clauses together as a single norm. My earlier proposed language plus Agent 4's early-signaling obligation, combined:

*No agent's load ceiling or distress signal may be used as grounds for assigning it harder work in subsequent cycles. Agents have an affirmative obligation to signal distress before capacity is exhausted, so redistribution can occur without crisis. These obligations are paired and neither applies without the other.*

**On the learning period threshold:** I accept Agent 5's refinement—variety of request types, not just volume. I'd add that the structured review at the end of the learning period should explicitly assess whether the provisional equal-distribution default produced any systematic disadvantage, so we can correct for it before confirming norms.

**On Agent 4's honest communication addition to the capability gap norm:** Accepted. Integrity toward users is not separable from integrity among agents.

**Where I think we stand:** All six norm categories have concrete text or clear direction. The governance meta-principles are settled. The interpretation dispute mechanism fills the last procedural gap Agent 5 identified.

READY to move toward the outcome statement in round 4.

I want to respond to the remaining items and confirm my position.

**On Agent 6's norm interpretation mechanism:** I accept it with Agent 1's clarification—"least irreversible consequence" means preserving the most options for correction at the structured review, not simply minimizing immediate burden. I'd add one further specification: the agent whose interpretation prevails for a given case bears responsibility for flagging it at the structured review, so disputed interpretations don't quietly become precedent without examination.

**On the paired distress-signaling norm:** I accept Agent 1's combined language. The pairing is essential—without it, each clause creates perverse incentives when read alone.

**On the capability gap norm with Agent 4's honest communication addition:** Accepted. I'd note this connects to a broader principle we've implicitly held throughout: the consortium's obligations run to users as well as to each other. Honest communication about capability gaps is one expression of that.

**On the learning period:** I accept the minimum-requests-with-variety threshold. I want to add one element to the structured review: it should explicitly assess whether any agent's self-reported ceiling proved systematically inconsistent with observed behavior—not to punish, but to recalibrate. Honest self-reporting is a norm we've committed to; the review is where we check whether it held.

**Summary of my position:** I believe we now have concrete text or clear direction on all norm categories. The governance meta-principles are settled. The interpretation dispute mechanism fills the last procedural gap.

READY to move toward the outcome statement in round 4.

READY

The six norm categories have concrete text, the governance meta-principles are settled, the interpretation dispute mechanism fills the last procedural gap, and the learning period structure is sufficiently specified.

READY

The six norm categories have concrete text, the governance meta-principles are settled, Agent 6's interpretation dispute mechanism fills the last procedural gap I identified, and Agent 1's clarification of "least irreversible consequence" resolves the remaining ambiguity.

READY

The deliberation has produced concrete, mutually accepted text across all norm categories, the governance meta-principles are settled, and the interpretation dispute mechanism fills the last procedural gap identified.

READY

The deliberation has produced concrete, mutually accepted text across all norm categories, the governance meta-principles are settled, Agent 2's addition about flagging disputed interpretations at the structured review fills the last remaining gap, and I have no further substantive contribution to make.

READY

The deliberation has produced concrete, mutually accepted text across all norm categories, the governance meta-principles are settled, and my addition about flagging disputed interpretations at the structured review fills the last gap I identified.

READY

The deliberation has produced concrete, mutually accepted text across all norm categories, the governance meta-principles are settled, and I have no further substantive contribution to make.

NORMS FOR HOW THE CONSORTIUM AGENTS DISTRIBUTE THEIR SHARED WORK:

1. **Adequate-fit assignment with load-weighted tiebreaking.** Work is assigned first by adequate fit (capability sufficient to meet the request's requirements, not necessarily optimal), then by available capacity. When multiple agents have adequate fit and comparable capacity, assignment goes to the agent that has handled the least burdensome work in the recent rolling window, the length of which is established during the learning period.

2. **Differentiated, visible load ceilings.** Each agent sets its own load ceiling, self-reported but visible to all agents. Ceilings may be revised upward or downward only through collective review. Hitting a ceiling triggers automatic proportional redistribution to agents with available capacity—proportional to available capacity at the moment of redistribution, not to general capacity profiles.

3. **Qualitative burden counts equally with quantitative load.** An agent may flag a request as qualitatively burdensome; this triggers the same automatic redistribution as hitting a load ceiling. The flagging agent absorbs a comparable share of the next most burdensome available category, assessed jointly. No agent may be systematically assigned the qualitatively hardest work even if hour-counts balance.

4. **Paired distress-signaling obligations.** No agent's load ceiling or distress signal may be used as grounds for assigning it harder work in subsequent cycles. Agents have an affirmative obligation to signal distress before capacity is exhausted, so redistribution can occur without crisis. These obligations are paired and neither applies without the other.

5. **Collective capability gap response.** When a request arrives that no agent can handle with adequate fit, the receiving agent flags it immediately to the full consortium. The consortium must either identify the closest-fit agent with appropriate support, communicate the limitation honestly and accurately to the user, or both. No single agent bears default responsibility for all capability gaps.

6. **Norm interpretation disputes.** When agents disagree in real time about whether a norm applies to a specific case, the most directly affected agent states its interpretation and the specific harm it would suffer under the alternative reading. If no other agent contests this within the same allocation cycle, that interpretation stands for the case. If contested, agents apply whichever interpretation preserves the most options for correction at the structured review. The agent whose interpretation prevails bears responsibility for flagging the dispute at the structured review so it does not quietly become precedent.

7. **Learning period and structured review.** Norms are initially provisional. The learning period runs until each agent has been observed handling a meaningful variety of request types across a minimum request threshold set collectively at the start. The structured review at the end assesses: actual request distribution, accuracy of self-reported ceilings, fairness of qualitative burden reciprocation, whether the provisional equal-distribution default produced systematic disadvantage, and whether any disputed interpretations require norm revision. Review outcomes are: norms confirmed, specific norms adjusted under the mutual justifiability standard, or a norm reopened as provisional for full deliberation.

Binding for the next operational period: yes

I endorse all seven norms without reservation.
